text: The development of efficient, stable and affordable electrodes for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is highly demanded for energy-related applications such as water splitting and rechargeable metal-air batteries. Here, we report for the first time the fabrication of hybrid porous nanowire arrays composed of strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon via direct carbonization of a cobalt-based metal-organic framework (MOF) platform grown on copper foil. The as-obtained $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4\text{C-NA}$ material presents a high surface area of $251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$ and a high carbon content of $52.1\text{ wt}\%$, making it a binder-free and integrated working electrode for the OER test. In alkaline solution, this electrode shows remarkable OER performance with low overpotential of $1.47\text{ V}$ at $10\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at only $1.52\text{ V}$, a Faradaic efficiency of $99.3\%$ and excellent stability for over $30\text{ h}$ operation. Importantly, the catalytic performance of this electrode even surpasses many reported noble metal-based and transition metal-based catalysts. The high OER performance of $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4\text{C-NA}$ material is attributed to the synergistic function of in-situ formed carbon and $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$, as well as the unique nanowire structure that accelerates charge and mass transportation and facilitates gas liberation. Furthermore, the $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4\text{C-NA}$ material also shows good activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) through a 4-electron pathway, making it highly promising for the reversible oxygen electrocatalysis in regenerative energy storage systems.
